description:
In Spring 2026, the Harvard Thursday Seminar is a graduate student-constructed semiadditivity learning seminar. We meet on the fifth day of the week at 3:30pm in Harvard SC 507.

Here are notes.
terms: E<sub>n</sub>-MODULE LOCAL SYSTEMS + f<sub>!</sub> ⊣ f<sup>*</sup> ⊣ f<sub>*</sub>

talks.title: Trivial Notions
talks.speaker: Matthew Niemiro (Harvard)
talks.description: The greatest stories do not begin with oo-category theory, and neither will ours. The germs of semiadditivity subsist willingly upon ordinary categories, ready for swab and cultivation. We will start our seminar by documenting some of the most basic semiadditive phenomena (in particular: bilimits, enrichments, norms, and ambidexterity for negative one and zero semiadditivity) as it is expressible in ordinary language. Then we will anticipate extensions of the theory. Finally, we will create a hostage situation to canvass speakers.

talks.title: I am NORMal and can be trusted with BOTH MY LEFT AND RIGHT HANDS
talks.speaker: Matthew Niemiro (Harvard)
talks.description: In this session, we will make the first definition of semiadditivity using ambidexterity and norms. The basic idea is that semiadditivity amounts to the existence of certain bilimits, hence it is a property concerning certain families F : X \to C where F and X are variable. This, in turn, may be understood inductively by analyzing the existence and invertibility of norm maps. But there's still a lot going on. The role of ambidexterity is to isolate that part of semiadditivity concerning X in a functorial and controllable manner, and this will help us run the machine. In particular, we will use ambidexterity to construct relative integration, a “higher” form of addition that will clarify the existence problem for norms.

talks.title: Cardinality and height
talks.speaker: Tyler Lane (Harvard)
talks.description: After reviewing the basics of semiadditivity, I will define semiadditive height and establish some of its basic properties. Afterwards, we will discuss the interactions between semiadditivity and chromatic homotopy theory. In particular, we will show that higher semiadditive structure dissolves the glue in the chromatic fracture square. We will also discuss modes.

talks.title: Spanish 101
talks.speaker: Sam Munoz-Echaniz (MIT)
talks.description: We previously defined semiadditivity using ambidexterity. The essential byproduct of ambidexterity was integration, which is a "higher" addition we used to construct norms. In this talk, we will see just how essential integration was: we will discuss the relationship between spans and addition, characterize semiadditivity in spanish, and discuss examples.

talks.title: K(n)-local semiadditivity
talks.speaker: Howard Beck (Northwestern)
talks.description: We prove the result originally due to Hopkins-Lurie that the category of K(n)-local spectra is \infty-semiadditive. Their proof relied on classical computations of the K(n)-cohomology of Eilenberg-MacLane anima by Ravenel-Wilson; we instead will present a more compact proof due to Ben-Moshe that uses more modern developments. It starts by bootstrapping off of classical Tate vanishing results in chromatically localized spectra (both Sp_T(n) and Sp_K(n)) due to Greenlees-Sadofsky, Hovey-Sadofsky, and Kuhn. We then induct on chromatic height using recent higher descent results on how algebraic K-theory interacts with higher semiadditivity (Ben-Moshe-Carmeli-Schlank-Yanovski), along with redshift phenomena of Lubin-Tate spectra (Yuan) and the Chromatic Nullstellensatz (Burklund-Schlank-Yuan). Along the way, we also show that the height n monochromatic categories Cat_{M_n} are also \infty-semiadditive.

talks.title: Parametry and Prejudice
talks.speaker: Lucas Piessevaux (Harvard)
talks.description: I will introduce the notion of parametrised semiadditivity with a view towards equivariant and chromatic homotopy theory. If time permits, I will discuss the role of span categories in this setting.
